g.data-narration: Welcome to this comprehensive lesson on the Data Encryption Standard, a foundational encryption algorithm in computer security.
g.text: A Comprehensive Guide to DES Algorithm

g.data-narration: DES is a symmetric-key block cipher developed by IBM in the 1970s. It encrypts data in 64-bit blocks using a 56-bit key.
g.text: A symmetric-key block cipher developed by IBM in the 1970s, approved by NIST.
Encrypts data in fixed 64-bit blocks using a 56-bit key for both encryption and decryption.

g.data-narration: DES was developed from IBM's Lucifer algorithm and became a federal standard in 1976, remaining in use until AES superseded it in 2001.
g.text: 1970s: IBM develops Lucifer algorithm → Modified to become DES
1976: Approved as federal standard | 2001: Superseded by AES

g.data-narration: The DES algorithm has three main stages: key expansion generates 16 sub-keys, encryption processes plaintext, and decryption reverses the process.
Key expansion takes the 56-bit key and generates 16 unique 48-bit sub-keys, one for each round of the encryption process.
The encryption process involves initial permutation, 16 rounds of the Feistel function, and final permutation to produce the ciphertext.
Each round splits the block into left and right halves. The right half goes through expansion, XOR with sub-key, S-box substitution, and permutation.
S-boxes are substitution boxes that transform 6-bit inputs into 4-bit outputs using non-linear transformations, providing cryptographic strength.
g.text: ⚡ S-boxes provide non-linearity - crucial for security

g.data-narration: DES has three main weaknesses: short 56-bit key vulnerable to brute force, weak S-boxes susceptible to cryptanalysis, and small 64-bit block size.
Three main attacks can be used against DES: brute-force tries all keys, differential analysis studies ciphertext differences, and linear analysis finds biases.
DES has been superseded by AES. AES supports 128, 192, or 256-bit keys with 128-bit blocks, while DES uses only 56-bit keys with 64-bit blocks.
Triple DES (3DES) applies DES three times with two or three keys, providing effective 112-bit security while maintaining backward compatibility.
Remember these key DES concepts: symmetric-key block cipher, 56-bit key, 64-bit blocks, 16 rounds, and the Feistel structure with S-box substitution.
After this lesson, you should understand DES history, algorithm stages, weaknesses, attacks, and the transition to modern encryption like AES.
